title: CDE Boundary Traffic ‚Äî Unexpected Corporate-to-Payment Flows

compliance.assurance_rationale: PCI DSS 1.3.1 ‚Äî Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that PCI DSS 1.3.1 is enforced ‚Äî Splunk UC-22.11.3: CDE Boundary Traffic ‚Äî Unexpected Corporate-to-Payment Flows.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.11.3' running on sourcetype pan:traffic and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted ‚Äî SME review required.

app: Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (2757)
spl:
index=netfw sourcetype="pan:traffic" action=allowed earliest=-24h
| lookup pci_corp_zones.csv zone AS src_zone OUTPUT corp_zone
| lookup pci_payment_subnets.csv cidr AS dest OUTPUT payment_tier
| where corp_zone="true" AND payment_tier="card_processing"
| stats count, values(dest_port) as ports, values(app) as apps by src, dest, rule
| where count>5
| sort - count
description: Highlights allowed sessions from general corporate segments into payment subnets so segmentation and NSC rules can be validated between assessments, not only during annual penetration tests.
value: Highlights allowed sessions from general corporate segments into payment subnets so segmentation and NSC rules can be validated between assessments, not only during annual penetration tests.
implementation: (1) Populate CIDR lookups from network diagrams approved for PCI. (2) Whitelist jump-host rules via `where NOT match(rule,"(?i)bastion|jump")`. (3) Feed hits to the segmentation validation runbook. (4) Map findings to Req 11 segmentation testing follow-up.
visualization: Heatmap (src_zone x dest_zone counts), table (top flows), Sankey optional for zone paths.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest | sort - count

detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (2757).
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=netfw` `sourcetype="pan:traffic"` ‚Äî `src`, `dest`, `src_zone`, `dest_zone`, `action`, `rule`; lookups `pci_corp_zones.csv`, `pci_payment_subnets.csv`.
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
(1) Populate CIDR lookups from network diagrams approved for PCI. (2) Whitelist jump-host rules via `where NOT match(rule,"(?i)bastion|jump")`. (3) Feed hits to the segmentation validation runbook. (4) Map findings to Req 11 segmentation testing follow-up.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=netfw sourcetype="pan:traffic" action=allowed earliest=-24h
| lookup pci_corp_zones.csv zone AS src_zone OUTPUT corp_zone
| lookup pci_payment_subnets.csv cidr AS dest OUTPUT payment_tier
| where corp_zone="true" AND payment_tier="card_processing"
| stats count, values(dest_port) as ports, values(app) as apps by src, dest, rule
| where count>5
| sort - count
```

Understanding this SPL

**CDE Boundary Traffic ‚Äî Unexpected Corporate-to-Payment Flows (PCI DSS Req 1.2.3, 1.3.1)** ‚Äî Highlights allowed sessions from general corporate segments into payment subnets so segmentation and NSC rules can be validated between assessments, not only during annual penetration tests.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=netfw` `sourcetype="pan:traffic"` ‚Äî `src`, `dest`, `src_zone`, `dest_zone`, `action`, `rule`; lookups `pci_corp_zones.csv`, `pci_payment_subnets.csv`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (2757). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: netfw; **sourcetype**: pan:traffic. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=netfw, sourcetype="pan:traffic", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
‚Ä¢ Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where corp_zone="true" AND payment_tier="card_processing"` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by src, dest, rule** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where count>5` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**CDE Boundary Traffic ‚Äî Unexpected Corporate-to-Payment Flows (PCI DSS Req 1.2.3, 1.3.1)** ‚Äî Highlights allowed sessions from general corporate segments into payment subnets so segmentation and NSC rules can be validated between assessments, not only during annual penetration tests.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=netfw` `sourcetype="pan:traffic"` ‚Äî `src`, `dest`, `src_zone`, `dest_zone`, `action`, `rule`; lookups `pci_corp_zones.csv`, `pci_payment_subnets.csv`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (2757). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Network_Traffic.All_Traffic` ‚Äî enable acceleration for that model.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Heatmap (src_zone x dest_zone counts), table (top flows), Sankey optional for zone paths.
